([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Österreichische Bundesbahnen* [[ˈøːstɐraɪçɪʃə ˈbʊndəsˌbaːnən]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German"), formally ***Österreichische Bundesbahnen-Holding [Aktiengesellschaft](/wiki/Aktiengesellschaft "Aktiengesellschaft")*** or ***ÖBB-Holding AG*** (lit. 'Austrian Federal Railways Holding Stock Company') and formerly the *Bundesbahnen Österreich* [[ˈbʊndəsˌbaːnən ˈøːstɐraɪç]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") or ***BBÖ*** [[beːbeːˈʔøː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German")), now commonly known as **ÖBB** ([[øːbeːˈbeː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German")), is the national railway company of [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), and the administrator of [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein")'s railways. The ÖBB group is owned entirely by the Republic of Austria, and is divided into several separate businesses that manage the infrastructure and operate passenger and freight services.

The **Austrian Federal Railways** ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Österreichische Bundesbahnen* [[ˈøːstɐraɪçɪʃə ˈbʊndəsˌbaːnən]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German"), formally ***Österreichische Bundesbahnen-Holding [Aktiengesellschaft](/wiki/Aktiengesellschaft "Aktiengesellschaft")*** or ***ÖBB-Holding AG*** (lit. 'Austrian Federal Railways Holding Stock Company') and formerly the *Bundesbahnen Österreich* [[ˈbʊndəsˌbaːnən ˈøːstɐraɪç]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") or ***BBÖ*** [[beːbeːˈʔøː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German")), now commonly known as **ÖBB** ([[øːbeːˈbeː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German")), is the national railway company of [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), and the administrator of [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein")'s railways. The ÖBB group is owned entirely by the Republic of Austria, and is divided into several separate businesses that manage the infrastructure and operate passenger and freight services.

The Austrian Federal Railways has had two discrete periods of existence. It was first formed in 1923, using the *Bundesbahn Österreich* name, as a successor to the [Imperial Royal Austrian State Railways](/wiki/Imperial_Royal_Austrian_State_Railways "Imperial Royal Austrian State Railways") (kkStB), but was incorporated into the *[Deutsche Reichsbahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Reichsbahn "Deutsche Reichsbahn")* during the 1938–1945 [Anschluss](/wiki/Anschluss "Anschluss"). It was reformed in 1947, under the slightly different name *Österreichische Bundesbahnen*, and remains in existence in this form.

Major changes currently being made to the Austrian railway network are the construction of the [Koralm Railway](/wiki/Koralm_Railway "Koralm Railway"), the [Semmering Base Tunnel](/wiki/Semmering_Base_Tunnel "Semmering Base Tunnel") and the [Brenner Base Tunnel](/wiki/Brenner_Base_Tunnel "Brenner Base Tunnel") connection with [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy").

[Eurobarometer](/wiki/Eurobarometer "Eurobarometer") surveys conducted in 2018 showed that satisfaction levels of Austrian rail passengers are among the highest in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") when it comes to punctuality, reliability and frequency of trains.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Furthermore, with their *[Nightjet](/wiki/Nightjet "Nightjet")* brand, ÖBB operates Europe's largest night train fleet.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Unlike other major railway companies in Europe that offer more flexible cancellation policies, ÖBB only offers two types of tickets: full-price tickets, and cheaper but non-exchangeable and non-refundable tickets.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austrian_Federal_Railways&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [Economy of Austria-Hungary § Railways](/wiki/Economy_of_Austria-Hungary#Railways "Economy of Austria-Hungary")

* 1882 – Gradual nationalisation of the railway network of the [Austro-Hungarian Empire](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Empire "Austro-Hungarian Empire") into the [Imperial Royal Austrian State Railways](/wiki/Imperial_Royal_Austrian_State_Railways "Imperial Royal Austrian State Railways") (*Kaiserlich-königliche österreichische Staatsbahnen*, kkStB). By the outbreak of the [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the only major railway in Austria to remain in private hands was the [Austrian Southern Railway](/wiki/Austrian_Southern_Railway "Austrian Southern Railway") (*Südbahn*).
* 1918 – After the break-up of the Austro-Hungarian Empire following the World War I, the Austrian rump of kkStB remained in state control under the name *Deutschösterreichische Staatsbahnen* (DÖStB), which was renamed the *Österreichische Staatsbahnen* (ÖStB) in 1919.
* 1923 – Foundation of the independent, commercial enterprise, the *Bundesbahn Österreich* (which used the abbreviation BBÖ, because ÖBB was already taken by the [Swiss](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") *[Oensingen-Balsthal-Bahn](/w/index.php?title=Oensingen-Balsthal-Bahn&action=edit&redlink=1 "Oensingen-Balsthal-Bahn (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oensingen-Balsthal-Bahn "de:Oensingen-Balsthal-Bahn")]*). This company took over the assets of the ÖStB and the *Südbahn*, together with other minor railways, like the [Lower Austrian State Railways](/w/index.php?title=Lower_Austrian_State_Railways&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lower Austrian State Railways (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nieder%C3%B6sterreichische_Landesbahnen "de:Niederösterreichische Landesbahnen")].
* 1938 – The [Anschluss](/wiki/Anschluss "Anschluss") of Austria into the [Third Reich](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"). The BBÖ was taken over by the [Deutsche Reichsbahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Reichsbahn "Deutsche Reichsbahn"). During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") about 41% of the Austrian railway network was destroyed.
* 1947 – The company was reformed using the slightly different name of *Österreichische Bundesbahnen* and the abbreviation ÖBB (by that time the Swiss private railway used the abbreviation SP for its goods wagons in international traffic, so its abbreviation ÖBB could now be appropriated) as a state-owned company. Their infrastructure was rebuilt and electrification was accelerated.
* 1969 – A new federal railway law was enacted. The ÖBB became a non-independent, economic entity, that was run as a branch of the government's industrial programme and remained entirely within the Federal budget.
* 1992 – The ÖBB were broken out of the federal budget and turned into company with its own legal status (a cross between a *GmbH* and an *AG* in Austrian commercial terms). The company is 100% owned by the Republic of Austria. This change had two primary aims: 1. It had to conform to EU rules on the admission of Austria into the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"). 2. The financial demand on the public purse was to be reduced as a result of improvements in efficiency and the pressure of competition.
* 2004 – The ÖBB were reorganised into ÖBB Holding AG and a number of operating subsidiaries. The holding company was to oversee the operations of the companies assigned to it, coordinate a coherent strategic approach and allocate tasks for the whole enterprise.[[4]](#cite_note-4)
* 1 January 2005 – The subsidiaries of ÖBB-Holding AG became autonomous and independent operationally.
* In 2012, ÖBB celebrated the 175th anniversary of the *[Nordbahn](/wiki/North_railway_(Austria) "North railway (Austria)")*, the earliest predecessor company marking the start of [rail transport in Austria](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Austria "Rail transport in Austria"). ÖBB CEO [Christian Kern](/wiki/Christian_Kern "Christian Kern") inaugurated an exhibition on the company's collaboration with [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), named "The Suppressed Years – Railway and National Socialism in Austria 1938–1945". He referred to that period as "the darkest chapter of our company history," adding that the company must accept this period as part of its legacy.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The exhibition later went on tour and was presented at the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament")'s [parliamentary building in Brussels](/wiki/Espace_L%C3%A9opold "Espace Léopold").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The Austrian rail system is largely electrified. Electrification of the system began in 1912 but did not reach an advanced state until the 1950s. The last steam locomotive in regular service on the [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge") network was retired in 1978.

The post-war laws related to the Austrian railways were the:

* *Eisenbahngesetz* (EisbG 1957),
* *Schieneninfrastrukturfinanzierungsgesetz* (SCHIG 1999),
* *Eisenbahnhochleistungsstreckengesetz* (HIG 1999) and
* *Bundesbahngesetz* (1992).

## Current structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austrian_Federal_Railways&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Current structure")]

By a law of August 2009, the organisational structure dating from 2005 was further modified; the railways are under the control of *ÖBB-Holding AG*, a holding company wholly owned by the Austrian state, under the [Ministry of Transport](/wiki/Ministry_of_Transport_(Austria) "Ministry of Transport (Austria)").[[9]](#cite_note-richter-9)

The holding company has a number of subsidiaries:[[10]](#cite_note-10)

* *ÖBB-Holding AG*
  + *ÖBB-Personenverkehr AG* (Passenger transport)
    - *[ÖBB-Postbus GmbH](/wiki/Postbus#Austria "Postbus")*
    - *ÖBB-Produktion GmbH* (50% shares)
    - *ÖBB-Technische Services GmbH* (75% shares) (railroad vehicle maintenance)
    - *Rail Tours Touristik GmbH*
    - *iMobility GmbH* (mobile app developer)
    - *ÖV Ticketshop GmbH* (online ticket distributor)
  + *[Rail Cargo Austria AG](/wiki/Rail_Cargo_Austria "Rail Cargo Austria") (RCA)* (Freight transport)
    - *Rail Cargo Hungaria Zrt.*
    - *ÖBB-Produktion GmbH* (50% shares)
    - *ÖBB-Technische Services GmbH* (25% shares)
    - *Rail Cargo Logistics – Austria GmbH*
    - *Rail Cargo Carrier Kft.*
    - *Rail Cargo Operator - CSKD s.r.o*
  + *ÖBB-Infrastruktur AG* (Infrastructure planning, management, and construction)
    - *ÖBB-Immobilienmanagement GmbH*
    - *Mungos Sicher & Sauber GmbH* (Security and Cleaning)
    - *Rail Equipment GmbH*
    - *WS Service GmbH* (51% shares)
    - *[Brenner Basistunnel BBT SE](/wiki/Brenner_Base_Tunnel "Brenner Base Tunnel")* (50% shares)
    - *Weichenwerk Wörth GmbH* (43.05% shares)
  + *ÖBB-Business Competence Center GmbH*
  + *ÖBB-Werbung GmbH*
  + *ÖBB-Finanzierungsservice GmbH*

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austrian_Federal_Railways&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

The infrastructure of the state-owned Austrian network is managed by ÖBB-Infrastruktur AG, which was formed from former infrastructure-related units including Brenner Eisenbahn GmbH. It now manages 9,740 km of track, 788 signal boxes, 247 tunnels, 6,207 bridges and eight [hydro-electric power (hep) stations](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity") for the 16.7 Hz electrification system, and two hep stations for 50 Hz power generation.

As of 2009 it employed 17,612 staff.[[9]](#cite_note-richter-9)

## Statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austrian_Federal_Railways&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Statistics")]

According to the Annual Report 2013, the company employs 39,513, there of 13,599 employees, 24,251 tenured employees [*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] and 1,663 apprentices. In 2013, ÖBB-Personenverkehr AG carried 469 million passengers of which 235 million were bus passengers.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
The ÖBB has

* 4,859 km (3,020 route miles); 72% electrified
* 1,128 train stations
* 1,093 locomotives
* 2,799 passenger vehicles
* 26,518 freight wagons
* 2,200 busses
* ÖBB's bus services travel 52,500,000 km (32,621,988 mi) per year.

* [Pannonia Railway](/wiki/Pannonia_Railway "Pannonia Railway") between Parndorf and Wulkaprodersdorf
* [Tauern Railway](/wiki/Tauern_Railway "Tauern Railway") from the Salzburg-Tyrol Railway at [Schwarzach](/wiki/Schwarzach_im_Pongau "Schwarzach im Pongau")/[Sankt Veit](/wiki/Sankt_Veit_im_Pongau "Sankt Veit im Pongau") to [Spittal an der Drau](/wiki/Spittal_an_der_Drau "Spittal an der Drau") via the [Tauern Railway Tunnel](/wiki/Tauern_Railway_Tunnel "Tauern Railway Tunnel")
* [Lower Inn Valley Railway](/wiki/Lower_Inn_Valley_Railway "Lower Inn Valley Railway") from the [German](/wiki/Germany "Germany") border near [Kufstein railway station](/wiki/Kufstein_railway_station "Kufstein railway station") via Wörgl Hauptbahnhof to [Innsbruck Hauptbahnhof](/wiki/Innsbruck_Hauptbahnhof "Innsbruck Hauptbahnhof"); to be relieved by the [New Lower Inn Valley railway](/wiki/New_Lower_Inn_Valley_railway "New Lower Inn Valley railway") line (under construction), part of the Trans-European [Berlin–Palermo railway axis](/wiki/Berlin%E2%80%93Palermo_railway_axis "Berlin–Palermo railway axis")
  + western continuation by the [Arlberg Railway](/wiki/Arlberg_Railway "Arlberg Railway") to [Bludenz railway station](/wiki/Bludenz_railway_station "Bludenz railway station") via the [Arlberg Railway Tunnel](/wiki/Arlberg_Railway_Tunnel "Arlberg Railway Tunnel")
  + northwestern continuation by the [Mittenwald Railway](/wiki/Mittenwald_Railway "Mittenwald Railway") to [Garmisch-Partenkirchen](/wiki/Garmisch-Partenkirchen "Garmisch-Partenkirchen"), Germany and the [Ausserfern Railway](/wiki/Ausserfern_Railway "Ausserfern Railway") to [Kempten Hauptbahnhof](/wiki/Kempten_Hauptbahnhof "Kempten Hauptbahnhof")
  + southern continuation by the [Brenner Railway](/wiki/Brenner_Railway "Brenner Railway") to [Bozen](/wiki/Bolzano "Bolzano"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") and [Verona](/wiki/Verona "Verona") via the [Innsbruck bypass](/wiki/Innsbruck_bypass "Innsbruck bypass") and the [Brenner Pass](/wiki/Brenner_Pass "Brenner Pass"), to be replaced by the [Brenner Base Tunnel](/wiki/Brenner_Base_Tunnel "Brenner Base Tunnel") (planned)

## Rail links to adjacent countries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austrian_Federal_Railways&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Rail links to adjacent countries")]

All neighbouring railways have the same gauge.

* [Czech Republic](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_the_Czech_Republic "Rail transport in the Czech Republic")  — voltage and frequency change to 25 kV 50 Hz AC
* [Germany](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Germany "Rail transport in Germany")  — same voltage and frequency 15 kV 16.7 Hz AC
* [Hungary](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Hungary "Rail transport in Hungary")  — voltage and frequency change to 25 kV 50 Hz AC
* [Italy](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Italy "Rail transport in Italy") — voltage and frequency change to 3 kV DC
* [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Liechtenstein "Rail transport in Liechtenstein")  — same voltage and frequency 15 kV 16.7 Hz AC
* [Slovakia](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Slovakia "Rail transport in Slovakia")  — voltage and frequency change to 25 kV 50 Hz AC
* [Slovenia](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Slovenia "Rail transport in Slovenia") — voltage and frequency change to 3 kV DC
* [Switzerland](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Switzerland "Rail transport in Switzerland")  — same voltage and frequency 15 kV 16.7 Hz AC
